summary: I was born in St. Louis, Missouri, in 1913, the third of three boys. After my father passed away during the 1918 flu epidemic, my mother moved us to Ocean Park, California, where I spent my childhood playing on the beach and attending local schools. I was active in sports like football and basketball during my school years and worked selling newspapers to help support my family. My early life was shaped by the vibrant beach community and various memorable experiences growing up in Southern California.

I was born in St. Louis, Missouri, in 1913 and lived there until about age five or six. My father worked in retail there before he died during the 1918 flu epidemic. One of my earliest memories is traveling by train from St. Louis to Los Angeles, which took about four days and three nights. Leaving St. Louis marked the beginning of a new chapter for me and my family in California.

After my father's death, my mother moved us to Ocean Park, California, where my great-aunt owned the Belclair Apartments on the oceanfront. Growing up there was wonderful; we lived on the beach and spent most of our time swimming, body surfing, and making surfboards. I attended Washington Grammar School there and enjoyed a lively childhood filled with sports and beach activities. The area was also a popular location for movie shoots, which was exciting to witness as a kid.

We arrived in Los Angeles after a long train trip from St. Louis, and it became the backdrop for much of my youth. I remember attending the 1932 Summer Olympics held in Los Angeles and visiting the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum with friends. The city was growing rapidly, with places like Wilshire Boulevard lined with eucalyptus trees. Los Angeles was where I experienced many important events and milestones during my childhood and adolescence.

I recall a New Year's Day outing to the Pacific Palisades with my mother and brothers. While there, a gentleman from London, England, took my picture because he wanted to show his friends how people dressed in California on January 1st. It was a unique experience that stood out during my childhood in the Los Angeles area.

A man from London, England, once took my picture at Pacific Palisades on New Year's Day to show his friends back home how Californians dressed in winter. I didn't understand it at the time, but it was interesting to have that connection to a faraway place. It was a small but memorable moment from my childhood.

I attended Washington Grammar School in Ocean Park after moving to California. It was part of my early education before I went on to John Adams Junior High and Santa Monica High School. The school was a place where I began to enjoy learning and meeting new people, a change from my earlier reluctance toward school in St. Louis.

After Washington Grammar School, I went to John Adams Junior High School. It was part of my progression through the local school system in California, where I became more involved in sports and social activities. This period helped me adjust to life in Southern California and build friendships.

I graduated from Santa Monica High School in January 1931, where I was vice president of my class. I played football, baseball, and basketball, and even tried pole vaulting, though I wasn’t very good at it. One of my teachers was Miss Ruth Judd, who was quite memorable and later connected to the infamous Winnie Ruth Judd case. My time at Santa Monica High was formative and full of both academic and extracurricular activities.

I had a newspaper route in Westwood, Los Angeles, where I sold about fifty-two papers. The houses were spread out, so I had to ride my bicycle quite a distance before starting my route. I remember one rainy day when I tried to ferry a subscriber’s daughter across a two-foot ravine on my bike, which ended with us tipping over. Westwood was part of my early work experience during the Depression era.

I remember watching the filming of The Sheik at Ocean Park when I was a child. It starred Rudolph Valentino, and they had tents set up on the beach for the production. It was fascinating to see how they used reflectors instead of flash cameras to light the scenes. This was one of the many movie shoots that took place near where I grew up.

Rudolph Valentino starred in The Sheik, which was filmed near where I lived in Ocean Park. As a kid, I found it exciting to watch him and the movie crew working on the beach. Valentino was a big name in those days, and seeing him in person was a memorable experience.

Charlie Chase was a comedian who did a lot of work down at Ocean Park when I was growing up. I remember seeing his comedy acts and how popular he was in the area. It was part of the lively entertainment scene that surrounded my childhood on the beach.

I remember the New York Giants playing in the 1921 Series, and it was always a headline in the newspapers I read as a kid. Baseball was a big deal to me even then, and following the Giants was part of my early sports interests. Those headlines were a common part of my childhood memories.

I recall the Graf Zeppelin flying over Ocean Park when I was in grammar school. The whole school was let out to watch it pass by, which was quite an event for us. It was a rare and exciting sight that left a lasting impression on me.

The Hindenburg disaster was another big news event during my school years. Like the Graf Zeppelin, it was something that captured the attention of everyone in Ocean Park. I remember the school letting us out to see the airships, and the tragedy of the Hindenburg was a somber moment in those times.

Clover Field was the airport near Ocean Park where the round-the-world fliers landed in the mid-1920s. Our school let us out to see the event, which was thrilling for a kid like me. Later, Clover Field became the site of the Douglas Aircraft Company, marking the area's growth and industrial importance.

The Douglas Aircraft Company was located at the site of the old Clover Field airport near where I grew up. I remember when the round-the-world fliers landed there, and later the area became an important industrial site for Douglas. It was interesting to see the transformation from a small airfield to a major aircraft company.

The 1932 Summer Olympics were held in Los Angeles, and I was lucky enough to attend some of the qualifying events with friends. We took a trolley car from Ocean Park to the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum, which was newly built and dedicated for the games. It was an exciting time, even though we accidentally took a wrong trolley car and got a tour of the city before getting home.

The Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum was built and dedicated for the 1932 Summer Olympics. I went there with friends to watch some of the events, and it was a grand and memorable experience. The Coliseum was a symbol of the city’s growth and pride during that period.

When I was growing up, Wilshire Boulevard was a forty-foot wide asphalt street lined with eucalyptus trees. It was quite different from the bustling, highly developed area it is today. I remember it as a quieter place during my school years in Southern California.

Miss Ruth Judd was one of my history teachers at Santa Monica High School around 1930. She was a blunt, learned woman who made a strong impression on me. Interestingly, she was related to Winnie Ruth Judd, who was involved in a notorious murder case shortly after I graduated.

Winnie Ruth Judd was infamous for decapitating two women and putting them in a trunk, a case that made headlines shortly after I graduated high school in 1931. She was related to my teacher, Miss Ruth Judd. I followed the case over the years and learned she was eventually released to society and worked as a caretaker for a couple.

After graduating high school, I attended Santa Monica Junior College, which was quite new at the time. I took a business course there for one year before moving on to work. The college has since grown and relocated, but it was an important step in my education during the early 1930s.

I worked for William F. Harrah in Venice, California, during the Depression. He ran a game called the Circle Game, which had thirty-three stools. It was one of my first jobs after college, and it helped me get through those tough economic times.

Venice, California, was where I worked for William F. Harrah in the Circle Game during the Depression. It was a lively area with many entertainment options, and working there gave me valuable experience. Venice was part of the greater Los Angeles area where I spent much of my youth and early adulthood.
